title: The case against efficient mourning
summary: Argue whether contemporary pressures to grieve quickly and resume normal functioning damage the psychological process of loss.
prompt: Write an argumentative essay (350–500 words) arguing that contemporary social and professional pressures to mourn quickly — short bereavement leave, pressure to 'move on' — damage rather than support people in loss. Refer to at least one concrete example (e.g. Japan's tradition of mourning periods, or the standard three-day bereavement leave common in many US and UK workplaces). Use a what-cleft sentence, a concession, and at least one passive construction. Take a clear position.
hintStarters: What the standard three-day bereavement leave in many workplaces signals is not compassion but…
Granted, not everyone needs the same duration of mourning, and it would be wrong to…
When grief is treated as a disruption to be managed rather than a process to be…

hintVocab.meaningVi: nghỉ phép tang gia
thương tiếc / để tang
bị thúc giục qua nỗi đau
rối loạn đau buồn kéo dài
bị coi là bệnh lý / bị coi như bệnh tật
văn hóa năng suất / văn hóa đề cao hiệu quả
tiếp tục hoạt động bình thường
bị kìm nén / bị đàn áp
đau buồn phức tạp / tang thương không giải quyết được
bị coi là không hợp lệ / bị tước đoạt tính hợp pháp
thời gian để tang
áp đặt khung thời gian
sự khép lại / sự chấp nhận và tiếp tục
được kỳ vọng phải hoạt động
nhịp độ của nỗi đau buồn
mất mát chưa được giải quyết

hintVocab.phrase: to be given permission to grieve
hintVocab.meaningVi: được cho phép để tang
sự thu mình về mặt tâm lý
bị coi là quá mức
tiêu hóa một sự mất mát / chấp nhận được mất mát
lịch trình của chính nỗi đau buồn

sampleText:
What the standard three-day bereavement leave in many US and UK workplaces signals is not compassion but arithmetic: grief has been assigned a duration, and when that duration expires, the grieving person is expected to function. Three days is the time typically allocated for losing a parent, a partner, a child — after which emails accumulate, meetings resume, and the loss is presumed to have been processed.

The social pressure to mourn efficiently is not confined to workplaces. It appears in the well-meaning advice to 'stay busy', in the friend who asks after two months whether you are 'feeling better yet', in the cultural assumption that protracted sadness is a symptom of weakness or pathology. Grief that outlasts the officially allotted time is treated as a problem to be solved rather than a process to be endured.

Granted, there is no universal mourning period that would suit everyone. Some people do find rapid re-engagement helpful; staying busy is for some a genuine coping strategy rather than avoidance. There is a real risk in insisting that grief must be long and visible to be legitimate — that too can become a form of pressure.

Even so, the current norm errs sharply in the other direction. Research on complicated grief has consistently found that people who are rushed through mourning — who are given no permission to withdraw, to be unproductive, to be visibly sad — are more likely to develop prolonged or unresolved grief later. The loss that cannot be absorbed at its own pace does not disappear; it accumulates. Japan's traditional mourning periods, which have varied historically but which acknowledge that the adjustment after a significant death takes months, not days, reflect a deeper understanding of what loss requires. By contrast, the three-day model treats grief as a disruption to be contained rather than a process to be respected.

What cannot be rushed without cost is the internal work of reorganising one's life around an absence. The person who returns to the office on day four, dry-eyed and apparently functional, has not finished grieving — they have been required to perform its completion. That performance comes at a price, one often paid years later in depression, in somatic illness, or in the sudden collapse that follows when the suppressed work of mourning can no longer be deferred.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: What-cleft to expose a contradiction between surface and reality
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What the standard three-day bereavement leave … signals is not compassion but arithmetic' — what-cleft đặt 'arithmetic' (từ gây sốc, thực) ở cuối câu, sau khi độc giả đã đọc qua 'not compassion'. Cấu trúc 'not X but Y' tạo ra sự phán xét đạo đức sắc bén.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What the standard three-day bereavement leave … signals is not compassion but arithmetic' uses a what-cleft to delay the key word ('arithmetic') until the end, after the reader has processed the contrast. 'Not X but Y' delivers a moral judgement in minimal words.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive to describe systemic expectations without blaming individuals
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'grief has been assigned a duration' and 'they have been required to perform its completion' — cả hai đều dùng bị động để chỉ ra rằng những kỳ vọng này đến từ một hệ thống hoặc văn hóa, không phải một cá nhân cụ thể. Điều này làm cho lập luận có tính hệ thống hơn là cá nhân.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Grief has been assigned a duration' and 'they have been required to perform its completion' both use passive constructions to locate responsibility in a system or culture rather than in any named individual, making the critique systemic rather than personal.

sampleTranslationVi:
Điều mà chế độ nghỉ phép tang gia ba ngày tiêu chuẩn ở nhiều nơi làm việc tại Mỹ và Anh phát đi tín hiệu không phải là lòng trắc ẩn mà là phép tính: nỗi đau buồn đã được giao cho một thời gian, và khi thời gian đó hết hạn, người đang đau buồn được kỳ vọng phải hoạt động. Ba ngày là thời gian thường được phân bổ cho việc mất cha mẹ, bạn đời, con cái — sau đó email tích lũy, cuộc họp tiếp tục, và sự mất mát được suy đoán là đã được xử lý.

Áp lực xã hội để tang thương hiệu quả không chỉ giới hạn ở nơi làm việc. Nó xuất hiện trong lời khuyên thiện chí để 'bận rộn', trong người bạn hỏi sau hai tháng liệu bạn có 'cảm thấy tốt hơn chưa', trong giả định văn hóa rằng nỗi buồn kéo dài là triệu chứng của sự yếu đuối hoặc bệnh lý. Nỗi đau buồn kéo dài hơn thời gian được phân bổ chính thức bị coi như vấn đề cần giải quyết chứ không phải là quá trình cần chịu đựng.

Phải công nhận, không có thời gian để tang phổ quát nào phù hợp với tất cả mọi người. Một số người thực sự thấy việc tái tham gia nhanh chóng là hữu ích; giữ bận rộn đối với một số người là chiến lược đối phó thực sự chứ không phải né tránh. Có rủi ro thực sự khi khăng khăng rằng nỗi đau buồn phải dài và có thể nhìn thấy để hợp lệ — điều đó cũng có thể trở thành một hình thức áp lực.

Dẫu vậy, quy chuẩn hiện tại sai lầm rõ rệt theo hướng ngược lại. Nghiên cứu về đau buồn phức tạp liên tục phát hiện rằng những người bị thúc giục qua tang thương — những người không được phép rút lui, không được phép kém năng suất, không được phép buồn bã rõ ràng — có nhiều khả năng phát triển đau buồn kéo dài hoặc không giải quyết được về sau. Sự mất mát không thể tiêu hóa theo nhịp độ của chính nó không biến mất; nó tích lũy. Các thời kỳ để tang truyền thống của Nhật Bản, trong lịch sử đã thay đổi nhưng thừa nhận rằng việc điều chỉnh sau một cái chết quan trọng mất nhiều tháng, không phải vài ngày, phản ánh sự hiểu biết sâu sắc hơn về những gì sự mất mát đòi hỏi. Ngược lại, mô hình ba ngày coi nỗi đau buồn như sự gián đoạn cần được kiểm soát chứ không phải là quá trình cần được tôn trọng.

Điều không thể vội vã mà không mất chi phí là công việc nội tâm của việc tái cơ cấu cuộc sống quanh một sự vắng mặt. Người trở lại văn phòng vào ngày thứ tư, khô mắt và có vẻ bình thường về mặt chức năng, chưa xong việc tang thương — họ đã bị yêu cầu thực hiện sự hoàn thành của nó. Màn trình diễn đó có cái giá phải trả, thường được trả nhiều năm sau trong trầm cảm, trong bệnh thân thể, hoặc trong sự sụp đổ đột ngột xảy ra khi công việc tang thương bị kìm nén không thể tiếp tục bị trì hoãn.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay's opening what-cleft argue the three-day bereavement leave communicates?
options: Compassion for employees' emotional needs.
An understanding of the complexity of loss.
An arithmetic assignment of duration to grief.
A flexible approach to workplace mourning.
mcQuestions.explanation: The cleft 'What … signals is not compassion but arithmetic' argues the policy communicates quantification, not genuine care.
mcQuestions.hint: The what-cleft contrasts two nouns — one positive, one critical.

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, what happens to grief that cannot be absorbed at its own pace?
options: It fades naturally over time.
It becomes easier to manage in social settings.
It accumulates and resurfaces as depression or somatic illness later.
It is released through work and productivity.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay states 'the loss that cannot be absorbed at its own pace does not disappear; it accumulates', and lists depression, somatic illness, and sudden collapse as consequences.
mcQuestions.hint: The final paragraph describes the consequences — what does the essay say happens 'years later'?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'prolonged grief disorder' refer to?
options: A grief period that has been officially extended by an employer.
A clinical condition where grief remains unresolved and interferes with functioning long after a loss.
A tradition in which mourning periods last for years.
The policy of extending bereavement leave beyond three days.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Prolonged grief disorder' is a psychological term for grief that remains intense and disabling well beyond expected recovery periods.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of what 'prolonged' and 'disorder' each mean separately, then combine them.

mcQuestions.prompt: How does the essay characterise Japan's traditional mourning periods?
options: As unnecessarily long and culturally specific.
As reflecting a deeper understanding that adjustment after loss takes months, not days.
As a policy introduced recently to compete with Western workplaces.
As the global standard all companies should adopt.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay presents Japan's mourning periods positively, as evidence that 'the adjustment after a significant death takes months, not days' — a contrast with the three-day model.
mcQuestions.hint: The essay introduces Japan to contrast with the three-day model — which direction is the contrast?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay mean by 'perform its completion'?
options: To complete a formal mourning ritual publicly.
To act as though grief is finished when it is not, because social or professional norms require it.
To write a formal completion report for bereavement leave.
To perform a ceremony marking the end of the mourning period.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Perform its completion' means acting as though grieving is done — appearing dry-eyed and functional — not because it actually is, but because it is required.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Perform' here means to act a role, not to do something genuinely.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's concession regarding mourning periods?
options: All workplaces should adopt the Japanese model immediately.
Three days is enough time for most people to grieve.
There is no universal mourning period; some people genuinely benefit from rapid re-engagement.
Grief is primarily a private matter and should not be discussed at work.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay grants that 'not everyone needs the same duration' and that 'staying busy is for some a genuine coping strategy' — this is the concession paragraph.
mcQuestions.hint: Re-read the paragraph beginning with 'Granted' — what valid point does the essay acknowledge?

options: A social condition caused by isolation.
Physical symptoms or illness that have psychological origins.
A severe form of depression requiring medication.
An illness common among elderly people.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Somatic' relates to the body — somatic illness refers to physical symptoms (headaches, pain, fatigue) that are caused or worsened by psychological stress or suppressed emotion.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Soma' is Greek for 'body' — somatic relates to physical experience.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive construction does the essay use to describe grief being treated as an arithmetic problem?
options: The social pressure to mourn efficiently is not confined to workplaces.
grief has been assigned a duration
staying busy is for some a genuine coping strategy
the adjustment after a significant death takes months
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Has been assigned' is present perfect passive — it places the act of assigning in a system rather than attributing it to any individual.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for a verb in the passive voice — 'has been + past participle'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to be pathologised' mean in the context of grief?
options: To be studied in a medical laboratory.
To be treated as a normal part of human experience.
To be classified as a medical disorder or sign of mental illness.
To be prescribed medication to ease emotional pain.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Pathologise' means to treat something as a disease or disorder — in the essay it means treating extended grief as a sign of illness rather than a natural human process.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Pathology' is the study of disease — what does 'pathologise' do to grief?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is implied by the phrase 'grief's own timetable'?
options: A schedule published by grief counsellors.
The natural, internal pace of mourning that varies between individuals and cannot be imposed externally.
The official bereavement calendar used by HR departments.
A timeline of grief stages that all people follow.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Grief's own timetable' implies that mourning has an internal rhythm that varies by person — it cannot be set by a workplace policy or social expectation.
mcQuestions.hint: The possessive 'grief's own' suggests grief has its own pace — whose timetable is it?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'Even so' to pivot from concession to main argument?
options: Even so, the current norm errs sharply in the other direction.
Even so, grief is very hard for many people in all cultures.
Even so, Japan has a long history of mourning traditions.
Even so, some employees appreciate having clear bereavement policies.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' in option A directly follows the concession paragraph, pivoting back to the essay's critical stance — 'despite what I just granted, the current system is still wrong'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Even so' should follow a concession and return to the main argument with a counterpoint.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'closure' mean in the context of grief?
options: The physical act of closing a coffin.
A sense of resolution or acceptance of a loss that allows one to move forward.
The official closing of a bereavement file at a workplace.
A ceremony held at the end of a formal mourning period.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Closure' in psychology refers to reaching a point of acceptance about a loss — a sense that the grieving process has been completed sufficiently to move forward.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Closure' is related to 'close' — something has been completed or resolved.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is 'complicated grief' as the essay uses the term?
options: Grief that involves multiple simultaneous losses.
Grief that occurs in difficult social circumstances.
Grief that remains unresolved, intense, or disabling beyond the expected recovery period.
Grief that is complicated by legal or financial issues.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Complicated grief' is a clinical term for grief that does not follow a typical recovery arc and interferes with daily functioning — the essay links it to being rushed.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Complicated' in clinical terms means something that does not resolve as expected.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase best captures the idea of social pressure being applied to a private experience?
options: the sudden collapse that follows when suppressed mourning can no longer be deferred
in the well-meaning advice to 'stay busy'
the adjustment after a significant death takes months, not days
the internal work of reorganising one's life around an absence
mcQuestions.explanation: The phrase 'well-meaning advice to stay busy' captures externally applied social pressure — it comes from others and enters the griever's private experience as an expectation.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase involves someone external directing the grieving person's behaviour?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains appropriate B2 formal register for an argumentative essay?
options: It's really unfair that people only get three days off when someone dies.
The loss that cannot be absorbed at its own pace does not disappear; it accumulates.
Bosses should give everyone way more time off when they're sad.
Grief is honestly so hard and people really need more help with it.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B uses precise vocabulary ('absorbed', 'accumulates'), a formal semi-colon structure, and an analytical, impersonal tone appropriate for B2 writing.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for precise vocabulary, no colloquial intensifiers, and controlled sentence structure.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay identify as the real cost of performing completed grief before it is complete?
options: Reduced workplace productivity and lower quality work.
Conflict with colleagues who do not understand grief.
Depression, somatic illness, or sudden collapse when suppressed mourning can no longer be deferred.
Legal consequences for employers who enforce short bereavement periods.
mcQuestions.explanation: The final paragraph lists the specific costs: 'depression, somatic illness, or the sudden collapse that follows when the suppressed work of mourning can no longer be deferred'.
mcQuestions.hint: The essay's final sentence names the costs explicitly.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Have you ever felt pressure to stop grieving and return to normal life before you were ready? What did that pressure come from — work, family, culture — and how did you respond to it?
